Cohle and Hart continue investigating Dora Lange's murder. They inform Dora's mother of her death; the mother mentions Dora recently visited claiming to attend church. They interview Dora's friend Carla, who confirms Dora was using drugs and mentioned a "shelter" near Spanish Lake. Under pressure, Major Quesada warns Hart and Cohle they may be replaced by three detectives from a new occult-crimes task force. Rust insults the task force members and is reprimanded; Marty pleads for more time and the Major relents, giving them a two-week deadline.

The investigation leads them to "The Ranch," a trailer park operating as a brothel where young girls work. There, one of Dora's friends gives them Dora's diary containing cryptic references to "the King in Yellow" and "Carcosa," along with a flyer for the Friends of Christ Revival. Cohle deduces that Hart is cheating on his wife Maggie with Lisa Tragnetti, a court stenographer, creating friction between the partners. The detectives find an abandoned, partially collapsed church with a painted mural of an antler-crowned female figure on the wall — eerily similar to how Dora's corpse was posed.

Meanwhile, Marty's family tensions grow. His in-laws visit, and Maggie expresses frustration about his constant absences. His daughters are discovered playing with dolls in a disturbing scene suggesting awareness of trauma or sexual content.

In 2012, Papania and Gilbough continue interviewing both men. Cohle reveals his backstory: his daughter Sofia was killed when struck by a vehicle while playing in their driveway, leading to the collapse of his marriage and his spiral into addiction. To avoid prosecution for killing a meth user who had injected his own infant with the drug, Cohle's superiors compelled him to serve as an undercover narcotics officer for four years, until he was hospitalized at Northshore Psychiatric Hospital after shooting three cartel members and being wounded. He still experiences chemical flashbacks from his time undercover.
